content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Most companies don’t set out to build an inaccessible website. It happens by default. Designers pick low-contrast colors, developers skip alt text under deadline pressure, and nobody tests with a screen reader until a customer complaint forces the issue. If you want to fix that before it becomes a problem, here’s a concrete path from “we have no idea where we stand” to “we have a working accessibility program.”
Step 1: Know What You’re Aiming For
Before touching any code, get familiar with the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG), the baseline referenced by most accessibility laws worldwide, including the ADA in the US and the European Accessibility Act. WCAG is organized around four principles — content should be perceivable, operable, understandable, and robust — with three conformance levels: A, AA, and AAA.
For nearly every business, WCAG 2.1 or 2.2 Level AA is the practical target. It’s the level referenced in most legal settlements and government procurement rules, and it covers the issues that affect the widest range of users without demanding extremes that rarely apply outside specialized contexts.
Write this target down. “We want to be accessible” is a mood. “We’re targeting WCAG 2.2 AA across our marketing site and checkout flow by Q4” is a project.
Step 2: Audit Before You Fix Anything
You can’t remediate what you haven’t measured. A proper audit combines three layers, and skipping any one of them leaves gaps.
Automated scanning — tools like axe, WAVE, or Lighthouse catch a meaningful share of common issues: missing alt attributes, contrast failures, empty links, missing form labels. Run these first because they’re fast and free, but don’t mistake a clean automated report for an accessible site — scanners are blind to anything that requires judgment, like whether alt text is actually meaningful.
Manual expert review — a trained auditor checks things scanners can’t judge. Does the alt text actually describe the image? Does the tab order make sense? Is the reading order logical once CSS is stripped away? Do custom dropdowns, modals, and carousels behave the way assistive technology expects?
Assistive technology testing — someone navigates the site using a keyboard only, then again with a screen reader such as NVDA, JAWS, or VoiceOver. This reveals the gap between “technically passes an automated check” and “a real person can complete a task.”
Choosing How to Get the Audit Done
Businesses generally pick from three approaches, and the right one depends on team size, budget, and how ongoing this needs to be: in-house with automated tools only, fastest and cheapest but catching only a fraction of real issues; independent manual auditors or consultants, a good middle ground for a one-time or annual deep audit when developers are on hand to implement fixes; or full-service accessibility agencies, which bundle audit, remediation, training, and monitoring and suit larger sites, regulated industries, or teams without spare developer capacity.
If this feels like more than your internal team can handle alongside its existing workload, this is usually the point where companies bring in outside help — dedicated web accessibility services can run this three-layer audit properly and hand you a prioritized findings report instead of a raw list of violations.
Step 3: Triage the Findings
A typical audit returns anywhere from dozens to hundreds of individual issues. Don’t fix them in the order they appear in the report. Sort by two factors instead:

• Severity — does the issue block a user from completing a task, such as a broken form or a keyboard trap, or does it just create friction, such as a suboptimal heading structure?

• Reach — does the issue live on a template used sitewide, or on one legacy page almost nobody visits?
High-severity, high-reach issues go first. A missing label on your primary “Submit Payment” button matters more than a slightly wrong heading level on an archived blog post from years ago.
Step 4: Remediate by Category
Once you have a prioritized list, most fixes fall into a handful of recurring buckets:

• Text alternatives — alt attributes on meaningful images, empty alt on decorative ones, transcripts for audio and video

• Color and contrast — text-to-background ratios meeting AA thresholds, never using color alone to convey meaning

• Keyboard access — every interactive element reachable via Tab, Enter, and Space, with a visible focus indicator

• Forms — every input tied to a visible label, clear error messages announced to screen readers

• Semantic structure — proper heading hierarchy, landmark regions, native HTML elements instead of generic divs styled to look like them

• Dynamic content — modals that trap focus correctly, live regions that announce updates, carousels built on accessible patterns
Fix at the component or template level wherever possible. Correcting a broken form field pattern once in your design system is worth more than patching the same bug on twenty individual pages.
Step 5: Build Accessibility Into How You Build
A one-time cleanup degrades the moment your team ships new features without the same standards. Bake accessibility into the workflow instead:

• Add automated accessibility checks to your CI pipeline so obvious regressions fail a build before they reach production

• Include accessibility criteria in your definition of done for any new UI component

• Give designers a reference palette of color combinations that already meet contrast requirements, so it isn’t a post-hoc fix

• Train content editors on writing meaningful link text and alt descriptions, since this work often happens outside the dev team entirely

• Maintain a small internal component library where accessible patterns are built once and reused, not rebuilt inconsistently by different teams
Writing an Accessibility Statement
An accessibility statement is a short, public page that tells visitors what standard you’re targeting, what’s been done so far, and how to report a problem. It signals intent to users and regulators, and gives you a defined channel for feedback instead of complaints landing in a random inbox.
A solid statement includes the conformance target (“we aim to meet WCAG 2.2 Level AA”), what’s been done so far, a direct contact method for reporting barriers, an expected response time, and the date it was last reviewed.
Keep the tone plain and honest. A statement that overclaims full compliance when gaps still exist creates more legal exposure than one that accurately describes ongoing work. Update it whenever a major audit or redesign changes the picture.
Handling Accessibility Feedback and Complaints
Publishing a contact method only helps if there’s a process behind it. Treat incoming reports the way you’d treat a security report: acknowledge quickly, triage seriously, and close the loop.

• Acknowledge fast — a quick reply confirming receipt, even before a fix is scoped, defuses most frustration

• Reproduce the issue — get the specific page, browser or app version, and assistive technology used, since these bugs are often environment-specific

• Triage with the same severity scale as your audit findings — a blocker reported by a real user jumps the queue ahead of routine backlog items

• Verify with assistive technology, not just a visual check, before marking it resolved

• Follow up with the reporter once it’s live, a step many companies skip and one that matters most to the person who raised it
A complaint handled well often prevents legal escalation. One ignored or answered with a form letter frequently pushes a user toward a formal filing instead.
Step 6: Monitor, Don’t “Finish”
Accessibility isn’t a certificate you earn once. Sites change constantly — new pages, new campaigns, and new third-party widgets embedded by marketing — and any of those can introduce fresh violations.

• Schedule a full re-audit annually, or after any major redesign

• Run automated scans monthly to catch regressions between full audits

• Keep the accessibility statement current and actually act on what comes in through it

• Track your VPAT (Voluntary Product Accessibility Template) if you sell to enterprise or government buyers, since procurement teams increasingly ask for one

• Review third-party scripts and embeds (chat widgets, booking tools, ad tags) periodically, since these render on your pages and count toward your compliance picture even though they sit outside your codebase
A Realistic Starting Point
If a full program feels like a lot to take on at once, start narrow: audit your highest-traffic pages and your core conversion flow, meaning signup, checkout, and contact forms, first. Fix what’s broken there, put basic CI checks in place so it doesn’t regress, and expand from there. Accessibility work compounds — every reusable component you fix correctly removes that bug from every future page built with it.
The businesses that struggle with accessibility usually aren’t the ones with the most complex sites. They’re the ones that never wrote down a target, never ran a real audit, and never assigned anyone ownership. Fix those three things first, and the technical work becomes a lot more manageable.
